one_liner: When did 10 stop looking like 10?
search_doc:
When did 10 stop looking like 10?
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-poster, headline-as-provocation, category-call-out, brand-platform-extension
Idea: Debunking the difference between skin care and anti-ageing products by exposing the problem of young girls using anti-ageing skin care.
Insight: Anti-ageing products are designed for adults, but TikTok-era 10-year-olds are now using them — making 10-year-olds no longer look or behave like 10-year-olds.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/BRONZE/10 vs 10 - Dove (Ogilvy, London _ Ogilvy, Sydney).jpeg

extracted.tagline: How Dove exposed the problem of young girls using anti-ageing skin care.
extracted.idea: Debunking the difference between skin care and anti-ageing products by exposing the problem of young girls using anti-ageing skin care.
execution: OOH billboard placement showing two side-by-side portraits of 10-year-old girls with the line 'When did 10 stop looking like 10?'
Dove 'Change Beauty' branded outdoor sites
Social conversation and parent engagement via TikTok content
Press coverage and earned media around the campaign
In-store / online retail tie-in encouraging conversation with caregivers
metrics: 209% increase in online conversation strength around the issue
1.5M organic views in the first week
99% positive sentiment on the caregiver resource

inferred.context: Young girls are increasingly buying and using adult anti-ageing skincare, fuelled by social-media beauty trends — Dove, the brand of real beauty, saw it had to call this out.
inferred.insight: Anti-ageing products are designed for adults, but TikTok-era 10-year-olds are now using them — making 10-year-olds no longer look or behave like 10-year-olds.
inferred.one_liner: When did 10 stop looking like 10?
inferred.qualitative_summary: Sparked a 209% surge in online conversation about young girls using anti-ageing products, drove 1.5M organic views in week one and earned 99% positive sentiment on Dove's parent-facing resource.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a real-world Dove billboard placement showing two close-up portraits of 10-year-old girls side by side with the question headline, set against a dark green park hedge backdrop; surrounded by social-post mockups on the left and stacked metric blocks on the right, with a press-logo strip and execution thumbnails along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Two side-by-side portraits of 10-year-old girls under the headline 'When did 10 stop looking like 10?' is a clean single-frame provocation about young girls using anti-ageing products, with the visual gap doing the conceptual work. The idea is sharp but more declarative than visually surprising — a strong headline-led poster rather than a category-redefining one.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's global personal-care brand whose 20-year 'Real Beauty' platform anchors beauty-standards activism aimed at girls and women, so calling out anti-ageing-product creep into 10-year-olds is exact platform extension rather than borrowed purpose. The cultural premise — that TikTok-era pre-teens are buying adult anti-ageing skincare — is observably true and topical, though the board does not cite category penetration data to anchor the scale of the phenomenon.

execution.rationale: OOH billboard placement and 'Change Beauty' branded sites carry the headline, with TikTok content for caregiver conversation and a retail tie-in for in-store discussion — coherent but the campaign reads more as an integrated OOH-and-social activation than a native print object. The single hero composition is well-shot but craft is closer to advocacy-poster competent than print-craft distinctive.

impact.rationale: +209% increase in online conversation strength is unanchored — '209% of what baseline?' is not disclosed on the board — and 1.5M organic views in week one is a modest engagement number for a global Dove platform extension. 99% positive sentiment on a caregiver resource is the surrogate metric a purpose campaign tends to report; the real business outcome a personal-care brand cares about (Dove sales, brand-affinity shift among parents) is not shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the headline-led provocation is on-brief for Dove's Real Beauty platform but the visual treatment is closer to an OOH billboard than native print craft, and the impact case relies on unbased percentages.

one_liner: 1943 photographs that look exactly like today's news.
search_doc:
1943 photographs that look exactly like today's news.
Mechanisms: archive-as-headline, temporal-ambiguity, purpose-driven-poster, craft-as-the-point
Idea: With this correlation established, prints were crafted that respond to the recent rise of warfare by campaigning for peace through the work of history's greatest war photographer.
Insight: Robert Capa's century-old war photographs are visually indistinguishable from contemporary news photography — proof that humanity has not moved beyond the conflicts he documented.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/BRONZE/1943 - Robert Capa (Innocean Berlin).webp

extracted.tagline: War has been contemporary for too long.
extracted.context: By observing that images captured by Robert Capa almost a century ago could still be mistaken for photos of ongoing wars, the centre selected visuals that better represent this ambiguity and displayed them with headlines that, through questions, provoke a comparison between our present and mankind's violent past of global-scale conflicts.
extracted.idea: With this correlation established, prints were crafted that respond to the recent rise of warfare by campaigning for peace through the work of history's greatest war photographer.
execution: Series of four black-and-white press prints pairing Capa war photographs with provocative headline questions
Headline '1943 OR 19:43?' over a soldier-in-smoke battlefield image
Headline 'HISTORY BOOKS OR TODAY'S NEWSPAPERS?' over a smoke-plume landscape
Headline 'A CENTURY, A DECADE, OR AN HOUR AGO?' over a civilians-in-rubble scene
Headline 'D-DAY OR YESTERDAY?' over a soldiers-in-grass advance
Each print signed with 'War has been contemporary for too long.' and the Robert Capa Contemporary Photography Center lock-up

inferred.insight: Robert Capa's century-old war photographs are visually indistinguishable from contemporary news photography — proof that humanity has not moved beyond the conflicts he documented.
inferred.one_liner: 1943 photographs that look exactly like today's news.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A craft-led press series for the Robert Capa Contemporary Photography Center that reframes the institution's archive as a peace-advocacy platform; the visual indistinguishability of 1940s and 2020s war imagery becomes the argument, without relying on quantified outcomes.

visual.style_description: Four black-and-white Capa war photographs arranged as a 2x2 grid on a textured grey paper background, each overlaid with a single bold white sans-serif question; a left-hand column carries the 'CAPA vs WAR' wordmark, a body-copy explanation and the standalone line 'WAR HAS BEEN CONTEMPORARY FOR TOO LONG.' under the Robert Capa Contemporary Photography Center mark.

review_reasons: No quantified metrics and no named press coverage on the board; impact signal is qualitative-only (craft + institutional positioning).

idea.rationale: The temporal-ambiguity device — '1943 OR 19:43?', 'D-DAY OR YESTERDAY?' — uses Capa's own war photography to ask whether contemporary news is indistinguishable from his archive, which is a clean, single-frame idea that lives or dies on the photograph behind it. It is a craft-led peace argument rather than a category-defining reframe, which is honest about what the work is.

strategy.rationale: The Robert Capa Contemporary Photography Center in Budapest is the Hungarian institution carrying Capa's legacy and championing photojournalism, so positioning it as a peace-advocacy platform via its own archive is exact mission-fit. The insight that 1940s war imagery is visually indistinguishable from current war coverage is an observable cultural truth made literal by the four side-by-side prints. The board offers no external data point or expert source to back the claim — the photographs are the argument, which is consistent with a craft entry but leaves Strategy unanchored quantitatively.

execution.rationale: Four-print series with a tight question-and-photograph grammar, consistent bold white sans-serif headlines on a textured grey backdrop, and a unifying line 'War has been contemporary for too long.' that signs every execution. The craft of the typography, the restraint of the single-question headline, and the curatorial selection from the Capa archive carry the multi-execution coherence; the work is native to a press page and would not translate to film without losing its argument.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified metrics and no named press coverage — no impressions, footfall, exhibition attendance, or donation lift for the Capa Center is shown. The brand's likely business outcome (visitor uptake or curatorial influence) is conspicuously absent, leaving the case to rest entirely on craft. The work is reviewed-flagged for exactly this absence; against tier-mate Print entries with explicit outcomes, the proof layer is thin.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the four-print Capa-vs-war system is genuine high-craft single-frame argumentation, but the board carries no quantified impact or earned-press evidence to lift it past Silver.

one_liner: Alphabet pasta becomes the folksong score.
search_doc:
Alphabet pasta becomes the folksong score.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, cultural-heritage-preservation, product-as-medium, branded-publication
Idea: The campaign collected alphabet pasta as a creative product to represent the verses of Medimurska popevka folksongs — a collection of five books, each containing a small package of alphabet pasta, was created, with the exact number of pasta letters for the verses of one folksong reprinted in the book.
Insight: Heritage folksongs survive only when the next generation sings them — and the bowl of pasta is one of the few moments families still spend together.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/BRONZE/A Soup To Remember - Marodi (Bruketa&Zinic&Grey, Zagreb).webp

extracted.tagline: Pasta Letters Book Collection
extracted.context: In Medimurje, Croatia, Medimurska popevka folksongs have been sung for generations. In 2018 they were inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. However, they will keep this status only if people continue singing them. Marodi, a local pasta manufacturer, believes that cooking brings generations together.
extracted.idea: The campaign collected alphabet pasta as a creative product to represent the verses of Medimurska popevka folksongs — a collection of five books, each containing a small package of alphabet pasta, was created, with the exact number of pasta letters for the verses of one folksong reprinted in the book.
execution: Limited edition of 200 books printed and packaged with alphabet pasta
Books used as a media and educational tool to engage with the target audience
Distributed through relevant local stakeholders, including Medimurje nursery schools
Promoted via key local promoters and younger generations as ambassadors
metrics: Social media campaign reached 90% of the Medimurje region
press_coverage: The Day of Popevka official holiday declared by the Government (November 28th)

inferred.insight: Heritage folksongs survive only when the next generation sings them — and the bowl of pasta is one of the few moments families still spend together.
inferred.one_liner: Alphabet pasta becomes the folksong score.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-printed Medimurje folksong verses as alphabet-pasta packages and books, triggering an officially-declared 'Day of Popevka' national holiday, 80% national reach and a 19% lift in Marodi pasta sales.

visual.style_description: Saturated yellow editorial spread with the campaign title set in a friendly serif; hero photograph of the 'Priča koja vrijedi svakog slova' book standing upright beside the five-book pasta-letter collection and a small pile of alphabet pasta, flanked by background / idea / execution copy on the left and a vertical 'results' column on the right.

idea.rationale: Re-scoring Medimurska popevka folksong verses as physical alphabet-pasta packaged inside a five-book collection — exact pasta-letter count per verse — is a genuinely novel branded-publication object that closes the gap between pantry product and intangible heritage. The idea works in print because each book IS the artefact, with the pasta as the score.

strategy.rationale: Marodi is a Medimurje-region Croatian pasta manufacturer whose product is structurally tied to family meals — making 'cooking brings generations together' a natural rather than borrowed brand mission. The strategic context is anchored on a verifiable cultural fact: Medimurska popevka was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2018, and the listing's continuation depends on continued practice. The insight that the family pasta meal is one of the rare moments to pass songs to the next generation is plausible and on-brand.

execution.rationale: A limited 200-book print run with a small package of alphabet pasta per book is craft-led but extremely small-scale, distributed via Medimurje nursery schools and local stakeholder networks with younger-generation ambassadors. The single yellow-and-serif art-direction system is coherent, but the campaign is essentially one execution scaled by hand-to-hand distribution rather than a multi-execution publication system.

impact.rationale: +19% Marodi pasta sales is a real business outcome and the right metric for a pasta brand — the absolute baseline isn't disclosed so '19% of what?' is partly open, but the metric is the correct one. 80% national-population reach and 90% Medimurje-regional reach are entrant-reported claims with no audit source on the board. The strongest cultural-footprint signal is the Croatian Government's declaration of 28 November as an official 'Day of Popevka' national holiday — a durable, non-paid policy outcome traceable to the campaign.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because a small-run printed pasta-letter book genuinely surfaced an endangered UNESCO folksong tradition and earned an official national holiday — held back by limited scale (200 copies) and unanchored reach percentages.

one_liner: Rum tastes better with Pepsi, not Coke.
search_doc:
Rum tastes better with Pepsi, not Coke.
Mechanisms: category-challenger, competitor-call-out, bar-context-hijack, taste-test-as-content
Idea: Pepsi wanted to own this mix-culture tension and ignite a conversation on Rum and Coke vs Rum and Pepsi. We caused these provocative, half-hid images across social media and SEO buys, high-profile print placements including The New York Post and OOH/printed bottle dispatches in the bars at the top of high-traffic streets.
Insight: Bartenders pour Coke with rum out of habit, not preference — so the conversation has to be reopened at the bar itself, in the half-second between rum bottle and mixer.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/BRONZE/Better with Pepsi - Rum (Alma DDB, Miami).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Pepsi might not be behind the bar, but it's always in the picture.
extracted.context: For over 100 years, Rum and Coke has been a staple at bartender tables; bar drinks have been priced 26% of participants prefer mixing rum with Pepsi compared to Coke.
extracted.idea: Pepsi wanted to own this mix-culture tension and ignite a conversation on Rum and Coke vs Rum and Pepsi. We caused these provocative, half-hid images across social media and SEO buys, high-profile print placements including The New York Post and OOH/printed bottle dispatches in the bars at the top of high-traffic streets.
execution: Print/photographic insertions in bars featuring bottle dispatches and bar imagery
Provocative tactical out-of-home and bar-table placements
Half-hidden Pepsi rum images surfaced across social media and SEO buys
High-profile print placements including The New York Post

inferred.context: Rum and Coke is the most iconic bar cocktail in the world — making Pepsi the perennial loser in mixer culture, despite consumer taste tests favouring Pepsi with rum.
inferred.insight: Bartenders pour Coke with rum out of habit, not preference — so the conversation has to be reopened at the bar itself, in the half-second between rum bottle and mixer.
inferred.one_liner: Rum tastes better with Pepsi, not Coke.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reignited the cola wars at the bar by reframing rum-and-Pepsi as the better pairing, earning 432M impressions and converting 589 bars from Coke to Pepsi.

visual.style_description: Dark, moody two-panel hero shot of a working bar counter showing two angled close-ups of rum bottles next to Pepsi cans, joined by a centre overlay of a Pepsi-branded rum bottle and the bold headline 'rum | better with pepsi'; press quotes run along the top, and a strip of bar/in-situ photo thumbnails plus two large metric callouts anchors the bottom.

idea.rationale: Reopening the cola wars at the bar — by pairing rum with Pepsi rather than Coke — is a category-honest competitive provocation, and the moody two-panel hero of two angled rum bottles next to Pepsi cans lands the gag in one frame. The idea is recognisably in the Pepsi vs. Coke lineage rather than a fresh reframe, which caps how distinctive it can feel in a craft-led category.

strategy.rationale: Pepsi is the world's perennial number-two cola whose brand has long been built on direct, taste-test-led challenger provocations against Coca-Cola, so a rum-pairing attack is exact mission-fit. The board cites '26% of participants prefer mixing rum with Pepsi compared to Coke' but the phrasing is garbled and the base — sample size, methodology, market — is not disclosed, so 26% of what is left open. The Rum-and-Coke cultural anchor is real; the preference data underpinning the reversal is unanchored.

execution.rationale: Print and OOH insertions in bars and on bar tables, half-hidden Pepsi rum images surfaced across social and SEO, plus high-profile press placements including The New York Post — the orchestration plants the message where the drink is actually ordered. The bar-context hijack is the strongest channel choice; the broader social-and-SEO layer is well-targeted to the bar-going audience but adds breadth more than craft to a print-led entry.

impact.rationale: +589 bars switched from Coke to Pepsi is the right business outcome for a beverage challenger — it is an absolute, distribution-level number rather than a surrogate metric, and exactly the proof Pepsi needs against Coke at the on-trade. 432M earned impressions is entrant-reported and unaudited, framed as a reach claim rather than a hard outcome, and the trade-press list (AdAge, Adweek, Yahoo, The Drum) is a credible directional signal. No share-of-mixer or volume figure is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the bar-switch behavioural outcome (589 venues) is a real challenger result, but the underlying preference data (26%) is unbased and the print idea sits inside a well-worn Pepsi-vs-Coke template.

one_liner: The black book that only a black light can read.
search_doc:
The black book that only a black light can read.
Mechanisms: media-as-product, invisible-ink-as-message, craft-as-the-point, purpose-driven-poster, cultural-iconography-co-opted
Idea: Design was the crucial element in balancing erasure and history, celebration and protest, darkness and light, in a unique and compelling visual identity. The solution was an intriguing book that seemingly had been erased. An innovative edition entirely printed on special ink that, under a special light — a black light — reveals all its beauty and content. Besides the book, special posters that became part of an exhibition were also created.
Insight: Black cultural and historical heritage has been so systematically erased that it can only be made visible through a literal act of revelation — printing it in invisible ink that only a black light can read.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/BRONZE/Black Lights -Bradesco (almapBBDO, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: By shedding light on the past, we can illuminate the future.
extracted.context: Brazil, the country with the largest black population outside the African continent, is also a country where black people are relegated to erasure: they make up 80% of violent deaths and 70% of school dropouts. A problem that dates back to the dawn of humanity violates the constitutional right to memory and robs an entire people of their most precious possession: their history.
extracted.idea: Design was the crucial element in balancing erasure and history, celebration and protest, darkness and light, in a unique and compelling visual identity. The solution was an intriguing book that seemingly had been erased. An innovative edition entirely printed on special ink that, under a special light — a black light — reveals all its beauty and content. Besides the book, special posters that became part of an exhibition were also created.
execution: A book printed entirely in special ink that only becomes legible under UV/black light
Special posters dedicated to erased black cultural and historical heritage, used as part of an exhibition
Illustrations and typography created by black artists
Exhibition rollout at the Museu Afro-Brasileiro in Salvador
Project supported by Bradesco Seguros in partnership with Project GOMA, a hub dedicated to accelerating the careers of black professionals
metrics: More than 5,800 hours of reading, research, and curatorial work
440 hours of exhibition time at the Museu Afro in Salvador

inferred.insight: Black cultural and historical heritage has been so systematically erased that it can only be made visible through a literal act of revelation — printing it in invisible ink that only a black light can read.
inferred.one_liner: The black book that only a black light can read.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A printed book and poster series for Bradesco Seguros and Museu Afro-Brasileiro that recovers four millennia of black history through 5,800 hours of curatorial work, with 440 hours of exhibition time anchoring the project in Salvador's permanent cultural infrastructure.

visual.style_description: Centre-stage product shot of the 'Luzes Negras' book on a deep navy/black background, its red and blue UV-revealed type glowing against the black cover; a top strip shows the book pages being illuminated by a black-light torch, the left column carries the campaign mark and a dense bilingual write-up of context/idea, the right column lists four numbered impact lines, and a bottom strip details craft sub-stories under headers like 'Erased Stories, Invisible Ink', 'Use of Typography' and 'Illustrations'.

idea.rationale: A book and poster series printed entirely in UV-reactive ink so the content of erased Black history is only legible under black light is a literal, single-frame metaphor for systemic erasure — the print mechanic IS the message. The conceit is strong enough to carry a single static page or exhibition wall without explanation.

strategy.rationale: Bradesco Seguros is the insurance arm of Brazil's second-largest private bank, with a long-running corporate-citizenship and education positioning, so a Black-history-recovery project carries some plausible brand-mission fit though it is more institutional patronage than a tight product-and-purpose lock. The strategic context is anchored on hard data cited on the board: Black Brazilians make up 80% of violent deaths and 70% of school dropouts in the country with the largest Black population outside Africa — sourced cultural facts that justify the project's existence. The brand–cause connection is mediated through Project GOMA partnership rather than being central to Bradesco's product offer.

execution.rationale: The UV-ink book, poster series and accompanying typography and illustrations are commissioned from Black artists, with a permanent-feel rollout at the Museu Afro-Brasileiro in Salvador for 440 hours of exhibition time — craft is high and ties the print object to a real cultural-infrastructure setting. The design system carries the conceit consistently across cover, interior typography and posters; the printed object is genuinely native to publishing rather than a digital piece dressed as one.

impact.rationale: 5,800 hours of curatorial work, '4 millennia of black history recovered' and 440 hours of exhibition time are process and footprint metrics rather than outcome metrics — they describe the project's depth, not its reach or behaviour change. The board carries no press coverage stack, no audience numbers, no perception-shift research, and no Bradesco brand-equity or product-side signal — the real business outcome for a sponsoring insurer is conspicuously absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the UV-ink-as-erasure mechanic is a category-strong single-frame print idea and the museum rollout is durable, but the impact case is entirely process-side (curator hours, history recovered) with no audience or brand-outcome evidence.

one_liner: The braid is the map of Black history.
search_doc:
The braid is the map of Black history.
Mechanisms: cultural-iconography-co-opted, body-as-canvas, purpose-driven-poster, legislative-advocacy
Idea: The campaign turns scalp braids into a printable map of Afro history — each braid pattern encodes a chapter of Black culture, ancestry and resistance — and is reproduced as posters, magazine inserts and editorial print.
Insight: Braids carry the stories of Afro-descendant communities and have always been a tool of memory and resistance.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/GOLD/Braided History - L_oréal de Puerto Rico (DDB Latin Puerto Rico).webp

extracted.tagline: The power of print to reshape law and fight discrimination.
extracted.context: In Puerto Rico, a growing number of individuals (mostly schoolchildren) face Afro-descendant racial discrimination, especially against natural hair textures and protective styles like braids and locs.
extracted.insight: Braids carry the stories of Afro-descendant communities and have always been a tool of memory and resistance.
extracted.idea: The campaign turns scalp braids into a printable map of Afro history — each braid pattern encodes a chapter of Black culture, ancestry and resistance — and is reproduced as posters, magazine inserts and editorial print.
execution: Hero photographic print of a braided scalp annotated as a historical map
Series of single-portrait Mizani prints showing different braid 'chapters'
Magazine and editorial print insertions
Out-of-home placements across Puerto Rico salons and public spaces

inferred.one_liner: The braid is the map of Black history.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Anchored a successful campaign behind Puerto Rico's anti-hair-discrimination law (PS 1282, approved April 2024) by reframing braids as a printed cultural archive; reached two million impressions with 98% positive sentiment.

visual.style_description: Hero macro photograph of a Black woman's intricately braided scalp annotated like a cartographic map, with callouts labelling each braid pattern as a chapter of Afro history; surrounded by a grid of supporting Mizani single-portrait prints on dark backgrounds and a bottom strip of in-context placement shots.

idea.rationale: Reading the scalp itself as a cartographic map of Afro-descendant history is a single-frame visual idea that lands instantly on a page: each braid pattern becomes a chapter, so the body literally carries the argument the law refused to recognise. The braid-as-archive reframing is genuinely fresh for the haircare category and natively print, working as one annotated portrait without any film translation.

strategy.rationale: Mizani is L'Oréal's professional haircare brand specialised in textured and curly hair, so advocacy for braids and locs is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose — the brand exists because those hair textures need dedicated products. The insight that braids encode memory and resistance for Afro-descendant communities is a documented cultural fact rather than a manufactured tension. The board states Puerto Rican schoolchildren face hair-based discrimination but does not cite the underlying study or incidence rate, leaving the quantitative side of the context unsourced.

execution.rationale: Hero macro photography of an annotated braided scalp anchors the system, extended through a series of single-portrait Mizani prints, magazine and editorial insertions, and salon and public-space OOH across Puerto Rico. The cartographic annotation device gives the series multi-execution coherence — every print reads as a new chapter of the same atlas — and the placement inside salons puts the message where the textured-hair audience actually transacts.

impact.rationale: The qualitative summary ties the work to the passage of Puerto Rico's anti-hair-discrimination law (PS 1282, April 2024), which is the strongest possible business-and-cultural outcome for an advocacy-led haircare brand — legislative change rather than impressions. The headline 2 million impressions and 98% positive sentiment are entrant-reported and unaudited, and '98% of how many sentiment-coded mentions?' is left open, so the numeric layer is softer than the legislative one. No sell-through or Mizani-specific business metric is shown, which is acceptable for a purpose campaign but caps the proof.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the braid-as-historical-map is a category-fresh, natively-print reframing that the jury could tie to an actual legislative win — PS 1282 — rather than to impressions alone.

one_liner: The billboard tells you where the sun is.
search_doc:
The billboard tells you where the sun is.
Mechanisms: data-as-media, real-time-billboard, weather-triggered-advertising, geolocation-targeting
Idea: Using real-time data, the campaign tracked every sunny spot in the city. Digital billboards showed exactly where and when to find those spots.
Insight: In low-sun markets like the UK, ice cream consumption depends on rare sunny moments — so the moment of sunshine, not the season, is the real sales trigger.

full_record.file_path: 2024/PRINT&PUBLISHING/GOLD/Find Your Summer - Magnum (Lola Mullenlowe, Madrid).jpeg

extracted.tagline: A billboard that tracks every sunny spot in the city.
extracted.context: Ice cream volume sales drop more than half in the UK during the low season, going from 140M litres to only 60M. The brand turned this into an opportunity, encouraging people to have an ice cream in winter while enjoying small moments of sunshine.
extracted.idea: Using real-time data, the campaign tracked every sunny spot in the city. Digital billboards showed exactly where and when to find those spots.
execution: Real-time data tracking of sunny spots in the city
Digital billboards displaying location and timing of sunshine (e.g. 'Gibbs St & Whitlock Rd, 12:51 pm to 1:12 pm')
Geo-located push notifications via beacons to nearby Magnum stores with a discount
3D city-map rendering showing tracked sunny zones
metrics: +66.3% sales than the average winter season

inferred.insight: In low-sun markets like the UK, ice cream consumption depends on rare sunny moments — so the moment of sunshine, not the season, is the real sales trigger.
inferred.one_liner: The billboard tells you where the sun is.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned winter weather data into a live media product, lifting sales by 66.3% versus the average winter season and earning broad coverage across major UK and trade press.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic hero photograph of a lone hooded figure standing in a narrow sunlit patch on a Madrid-like street, framed as if shot from a doorway; the rest of the board is a black wrap with thin text columns on the left and right, a 3D city-grid CGI snapshot, app-notification mockup, OOH placement thumbnails, and a dense press logo strip along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Turning real-time sunshine tracking into the media itself — a billboard that tells you exactly where the sun is at 12:51pm on a specific corner — is a smart inversion of the seasonal ice-cream problem and lands with a single, image-clear sun-patch hero shot. The idea is more native to digital OOH than to a static page, which is its mild softness within a strictly Print frame.

strategy.rationale: Magnum is Unilever's premium adult ice-cream brand whose 'Pleasure' platform anchors permission-to-indulge messaging year-round, so attacking the winter sales trough by reframing sunshine as a pleasure cue is on-mission rather than borrowed. The strategic problem is plainly anchored: UK ice-cream volume drops from 140M litres to 60M in winter — a credible, sourced category fact that makes the campaign's reason-to-exist tangible. Insight is implicit (sun-moments matter more than seasons) and not separately data-backed beyond the volume drop.

execution.rationale: Live weather and sun-position data feeds digital billboards with precise spot-and-time call-outs ('Gibbs St & Whitlock Rd, 12:51 pm to 1:12 pm'), with beacon-triggered geo push-notifications to nearby Magnum stockists carrying a discount — a clean stack from awareness to redemption. 3D city-rendering, app mock-ups and OOH placement thumbnails on the board are coherent and the system extends across multiple urban contexts.

impact.rationale: +66.3% sales versus the average winter season is a credible business-outcome number — a like-for-like seasonal comparison and the right metric for an ice-cream brand fighting the off-season — though the absolute baseline volume is not given on the board, so '66.3% of how much?' remains partly open. Press pickup is dense across UK tier-one (The Times, Guardian, Telegraph, Evening Standard, Mirror) and trade (Adweek, AdAge, Campaign, Fast Company), all entrant-reported but a credible breadth signal.

one_liner: The crushed can IS the recycling call-to-action.
search_doc:
The crushed can IS the recycling call-to-action.
Mechanisms: brand-icon-recontextualisation, guideline-violation-as-message, everyday-object-transformation, purpose-driven-poster
Idea: The Coca-Cola logo is recognised by 94% of the world's population. The campaign uses the brand's famous identity to telegraph the importance of recycling: Coca-Cola broke its strict brand guidelines and crushed its iconic logo to inspire people to recycle every can. Cans were crushed by hand, by recycling-plant press and by vacuum; the individual crushed logos were extracted from the cans and the call-to-action 'RECYCLE ME' is taken from the side of the can. Because no two crushed cans are identical, neither are the executions.
Insight: A logo recognised by 94% of the world is powerful enough that it can be deliberately deformed and still read as Coca-Cola — making the brand's most protected asset the perfect medium to model recycling behaviour.

The 'RECYCLE ME' lockup lifted from the side of the actual can closes the loop between brand identity and behaviour cue with no extra art-direction load.

strategy.rationale: Coca-Cola is the world's most-distributed soft drink brand, with public sustainability commitments to make 100% of packaging recyclable by 2025 and to collect a bottle or can for every one it sells by 2030 — so a recycling provocation built on its own packaging is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The strategic premise — that a logo recognised by 94% of the world can survive deformation and still read as Coca-Cola — is the rare insight that is empirically true and load-bearing for the creative. The 94% recognition figure is entrant-cited rather than independently sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: Three different physical crushing methods (hand, recycling-press, vacuum) generate genuinely distinct logo deformations, giving an entire campaign system from one mechanic; the multilingual lockups (RECYCLE ME / RECICLAME / RECICLE-ME) carry the same idea cleanly across markets. Single-frame craft is high — the can is hero, background is flat brand red, no decorative noise — and the bottom strip of eleven variants proves the system's scalability rather than relying on one lucky shot.

impact.rationale: Earned coverage across AdAge, Fast Company, Adweek and TAXI is real industry endorsement and a Fernando Machado personal pickup adds creative-leader credibility, though all press is entrant-curated and the board carries no audited reach figure. The headline 94% recognition number is the campaign's strategic input, not an outcome metric — recycling behaviour-change, sell-in lift or sustainability-perception data are conspicuously absent, so the cultural footprint is asserted via critical acclaim rather than measured business outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because Coca-Cola willingly sacrifices its most-protected brand asset and turns each crushed can into a unique, self-documenting recycling poster — the medium IS the message, in a way only a 94%-recognised logo can pull off.

Mechanisms: media-as-product, citizen-co-production, press-freedom-advocacy, rights-circumvention
Idea: Initiate a grassroots movement where ordinary people became part of the press for journalists' free, transforming Nicaraguan home into printers, transporting the word through social media and pricing copies themselves. strategy.rationale: La Prensa is Nicaragua's century-old newspaper of record, whose entire institutional purpose is independent journalism, so a campaign about keeping the press alive under dictatorship is identity-fit at the deepest possible level. The strategic context is anchored on a verifiable political fact stated on the board: since 2018 the Nicaraguan regime has criminalised the free press, leaving the country as the only nation in the world without a printed newspaper. The legal mechanism — using Rio San Juan navigation rights to print outside the regime's reach — is a real geopolitical lever, not a creative metaphor.

execution.rationale: A boat-based mobile printing operation on the Rio San Juan, a distributed network of citizen 'printing rooms' inside Nicaraguan homes and hand-to-hand distribution amplified through social create a clean three-stage system from production to readership. The board carries documentary press photography rather than a stylised print-craft system — coherent but more reportage layout than print-craft showcase.

impact.rationale: Going 'from 0 to thousands of printing rooms', +350k new readers and a +120% readership increase are the right business outcomes for a newspaper restarting under censorship — though '120% of what baseline?' is not given, the absolute reader figure (350k) anchors the percentage. why_it_won.verdict: Bronze for a campaign whose distributed-printing mechanic and global press-freedom resonance read closer to Silver — the jury appears to have been harsher on the print-craft polish than on the underlying idea.

idea.rationale: Inverting 130 years of trademark policing into public endorsement of bootleg shopfront logos is a strong category-defining reframe — the very thing the brand normally litigates becomes the campaign asset. strategy.rationale: Coca-Cola is the world's most-distributed soft drink and a brand whose identity has been built on disciplined consistency of its Spencerian script logo for over a century, so a public concession that unauthorised local versions are flattery rather than infringement is a meaningful, on-brief shift in posture for that specific brand. The insight — that imitation is the most authentic affection signal — is plausible and observable in any bodega-heavy market, and the board frames it as a cultural truth the brand had been mis-reading. The veracity of 'thousands of unofficial versions' is not numerically sourced, but the photographic evidence across multiple continents stands in for the proof.

execution.rationale: The system runs as a global photographic print platform: a lead Coca-Cola photographer documents bodega owners with their hand-painted logos, with executions extending across Australia, India, Indonesia, South Africa and Brazil, and is amplified on walls, delivery trucks and coolers as native retail media. The print mosaic and consistent Coca-Cola red carry multi-execution coherence across very different vernaculars, and the choice to honour the bodega owner by name keeps the work documentary rather than appropriative.

impact.rationale: The 95% positive brand sentiment is entrant-reported and unanchored — '95% of how many tracked mentions, in which markets?' is not on the board — and 3.7 billion reach is a surrogate impressions figure rather than a sales or distribution outcome. Tier-one trade press from AdAge and Yahoo is a credible signal of earned conversation, but the real business outcome a global cola brand cares about — volume, share, or shopkeeper-partnership uptake — is conspicuously absent. The cultural footprint of repositioning bootleg logos sits stronger than the quantitative case.

visual.style_description: A hero photograph of the 'Unfinished.' hardcover book — half-tone portrait of Ellen White cropping off the page edge — anchors the left two-thirds of the board against a soft grey gradient; the right half is a tight editorial grid of book-spread photography, behind-the-scenes printing and parliamentary imagery, organised under Insight / Idea / Impact headers with press quotes and a logo bar at the bottom.

idea.rationale: An autobiography of England's greatest-ever women's goalscorer that physically cuts off mid-teenage-years turns a printed object into a protest artefact, and the truncation lands instantly when you hold or photograph the book. The print mechanic — a real book with a real abrupt blank — is genuinely native to publishing rather than a digital story compressed into a page.

strategy.rationale: EE is BT Group's UK mobile network whose 'Hope United' anti-online-hate platform has run since the 2021 Euros, so a campaign about online abuse driving girls out of sport is direct platform extension rather than borrowed purpose. The insight is anchored on a specific data point cited on the board — '44% of UK teenage girls have personally experienced sport-related hate online, forcing 121 young women across the country to give up on their sporting stories every single day' — a sourced, scoped statistic that does the strategic work. The 121-per-day figure is entrant-presented and not externally cited on the board.

execution.rationale: A single printed autobiography is co-authored with Ellen White, distributed into bookshops and libraries, and amplified through UK tier-one press (Daily Mail, Mirror, BBC, Sky Sports News, Forbes, Channel 4) into a Government endorsement from the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology with a public commitment toward Online Safety Act protections. The orchestration moves cleanly from craft object to media moment to legislative lever — coherent, but the print object itself is one execution rather than a system.

impact.rationale: 492M impressions in 10 days is entrant-reported / unaudited but a credible reach signal given the press breadth, and a 122% increase in web visits is a directional engagement proxy — '122% of what baseline?' is not disclosed on the board so the lift is unanchored. The strongest impact signal is non-numerical: a sitting UK Cabinet Minister publicly endorsing the work and committing to legislative protections for women online — a rare cultural-footprint outcome for a telco brand campaign.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the abruptly-ending book is a genuinely native print object that converted a single craft artefact into UK Government endorsement and an Online Safety Act commitment — held back from Gold by impact metrics that rely on unanchored percentages and reach claims.

one_liner: The candidate aims at the shooting in his own district.
search_doc:
The candidate aims at the shooting in his own district.
Mechanisms: side-by-side-juxtaposition, found-photography-pairing, purpose-driven-poster, legislative-advocacy
Idea: Place published news photos of U.S. politicians posing with assault rifles next to news photos from the same newspaper of mass shootings that took place in their own districts during their time in office — printed side by side as a single press spread so the politician is literally aiming the gun at the consequence of their vote.
Insight: Voters rarely connect the gun-rights stance of their elected officials with the specific mass shootings that happen in their own districts — placing the two side by side forces the link.

extracted.tagline: Voting for a candidate who is against gun violence prevention has consequences. This election, choose wisely.
extracted.context: Change the Ref wanted to raise awareness about the importance of holding elected candidates accountable for the mass shootings that happen under their watch — exposing politicians who opposed gun safety laws for the consequences of their lack of support.

execution: Side-by-side press spreads pairing politicians posing with assault rifles and mass-shootings in their district

inferred.insight: Voters rarely connect the gun-rights stance of their elected officials with the specific mass shootings that happen in their own districts — placing the two side by side forces the link.
inferred.one_liner: The candidate aims at the shooting in his own district.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repurposed published news photography into accusatory political print spreads that pair pro-gun politicians with mass shootings in their own districts; drove almost 30,000 voter pledges against the named candidates.

visual.style_description: Editorial gallery layout: two large framed press spreads on a cream background, each pairing a documentary photo of a U.S. politician aiming an assault rifle with a parallel photo of a mass shooting in the same state; left column is a typewriter-style 'Background / Idea' write-up on black, bottom strip mosaics more candidate spreads, politician headshots and a 'Make A Pledge' form.

idea.rationale: Pairing a politician's own press photo with assault rifle next to a press photo of a mass shooting in the same district, on a single facing-page spread, makes the politician literally aim at the consequences of their own vote. The juxtaposition is found-photography rather than constructed imagery, which gives it documentary weight that a manufactured scene could not match.

strategy.rationale: Change the Ref is the gun-safety advocacy organisation founded by Manuel and Patricia Oliver after their son Joaquin was killed in the Parkland shooting, so a press spread that holds named legislators accountable to specific shootings in their districts is core to the organisation's mission, not borrowed purpose. The insight that voters rarely connect their representatives' pro-gun votes with shootings in their own district is plausible and politically actionable. The board names no specific election or constituency-level data to back the connection statistically — the spreads are themselves the proof.

execution.rationale: The execution is a print spread system inserted into newspapers, with QR codes routing readers to a 'Make a Pledge' microsite, extended through outdoor projection and a gallery-style exhibition of grouped portraits. Using found photography from the same newspaper that runs the ad gives the work an internal closure — the medium is auditing its own archive — and the gallery extension keeps the print system coherent across formats.

impact.rationale: 29,766 pledges is an absolute, unambiguous behavioural number — no percentage gymnastics — and is the right outcome for a voter-mobilisation campaign rather than impressions. The board carries no named press coverage and no electoral outcome data (whether any of the targeted candidates lost), so the case stops at pledges rather than reaching voting behaviour or legislative consequence. The lack of broader earned-media evidence on the board caps the impact ceiling.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the found-photography juxtaposition is a genuinely powerful single-spread idea on a credibly on-mission brand, but the impact case stops at pledges with no proven electoral or legislative knock-on.
